topic: The role of autonomous AI agents in scientific discovery and academic knowledge synthesis
final_review:
The role of autonomous AI agents in scientific discovery and academic knowledge synthesis is a rapidly evolving field, with various studies exploring its potential and challenges. Recent research has focused on developing autonomous AI agents for specific domains, such as spatial biology (Wang et al., 2025) and scientific discovery (Ghareeb et al., 2025). These agents have the potential to automate tasks, synthesize knowledge, and facilitate new discoveries.

However, the integration of prior knowledge into learning systems is crucial for the success of autonomous AI agents (von Rueden et al., 2021). This highlights the need for informed machine learning approaches that can incorporate domain expertise and causal representation learning (Schölkopf et al., 2021). The application of deep learning techniques can also enhance the capabilities of autonomous AI agents.

Despite the potential benefits, there are concerns about the implications of autonomous AI agents on research, practice, and policy (Dwivedi et al., 2019; Dwivedi et al., 2023). The increasing use of generative conversational AI raises questions about authorship and the role of human researchers (Dwivedi et al., 2023). Furthermore, the development of autonomous AI agents requires a multidisciplinary approach, incorporating perspectives from education (Chen et al., 2020; Zawacki‐Richter et al., 2019), social sciences, and technology.

The current literature highlights the need for further research on the applications, challenges, and implications of autonomous AI agents in scientific discovery and academic knowledge synthesis. There is a research gap in exploring the potential of autonomous AI agents in facilitating collaboration between humans and machines, as well as their impact on the future of research and education.

References:
Alzubaidi, L., Zhang, J., Humaidi, A. J., et al. (2021) Review of deep learning: concepts, CNN architectures, challenges, applications, future directions.
Chen, L., Chen, P., Lin, Z. (2020) Artificial Intelligence in Education: A Review.
Dwivedi, Y. K., Hughes, L., Ismagilova, E., et al. (2019) Artificial Intelligence (AI): Multidisciplinary perspectives on emerging challenges, opportunities, and agenda for research, practice and policy.
Dwivedi, Y. K., Kshetri, N., Hughes, L., et al. (2023) Opinion Paper: “So what if ChatGPT wrote it?” Multidisciplinary perspectives on opportunities, challenges and implications of generative conversational AI for research, practice and policy.
Ghareeb, A., Chang, B., Mitchener, L., et al. (2025) Robin: A multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery.
Sarker, I. H. (2021) Deep Learning: A Comprehensive Overview on Techniques, Taxonomy, Applications and Research Directions.
Schölkopf, B., Locatello, F., Bauer, S., et al. (2021) Toward Causal Representation Learning.
von Rueden, L., Mayer, S., Beckh, K., et al. (2021) Informed Machine Learning - A Taxonomy and Survey of Integrating Prior Knowledge into Learning Systems.
Wang, H., He, Y., Coelho, P. P., et al. (2025) SpatialAgent: An autonomous AI agent for spatial biology.
Zawacki‐Richter, O., Marín, V. I., Bond, M., et al. (2019) Systematic review of research on artificial intelligence applications in higher education – where are the educators?
